Author: Mary Paskhaver
Title: What Is a Monad in Non-Technical Terms?
Text:
Member-only story
What Is a Monad in Non-Technical Terms?
Functional programming for non-programmers
In 2009, Professor Brent Yorgey criticized tutorials that defined monads abstractly. His popular example was burritos.
You shouldn’t teach monads by saying they’re like burritos, he said. Doing so prevents programmers from studying monads how they’re supposed to: by investigating code that makes and uses them.
Yorgey argued that programmers have to know what’s concrete about monads — for example, the types and functions involved — before they can come up with intuitive abstractions that help them understand their particulars.
But what if you’re not a programmer?
What if you are, for example, the relative of a snooty functional programmer who likes to talk about monads in casual dinner conversation?
Then you’ve come to the right place!
In this article, I will try to explain monads, from my experience with Haskell, in non-technical terms. That is, without code.
Because this tutorial is non-technical, it will inherently miss some finer details. But my goal is to capture the big picture.
I may fail here, but this is a learning opportunity for me as well. Let’s dive into it!
